q: The outermost shell electrons of atoms are the basis for understanding the joining of atoms.
The principles of filling electrons in the atomic orbitals include the Aufbau principle, Pauli's exclusion principle, and Hund's rule.
The $s$ subshell has three dumbbell-shaped orbitals known as $p_x$, $p_y$, and $p_z$.
According to the Aufbau principle, electrons are filled in the higher energy atomic orbitals before filling lower energy ones.
Aufbau is a German word that means building up.
An electron can spin in only two directions, represented with arrows as $\uparrow$ or $\downarrow$.
Pauli's exclusion principle states that two electrons with the same spin can occupy the same orbital.
Hund's rule states that every orbital in a subshell is singly occupied with one electron before any one orbital is doubly occupied.
Valence electrons are the electrons in the outermost shells of an atom.
Whenever a chemical bond forms, atoms lose, gain, or share electrons in order to achieve the stable electronic structures of noble gases.
The octet rule applies strictly to all elements, including $d$-block elements.
Lewis symbols for elements represent valence electrons as dots.
Electronegativity ($EN$) is a measure of the ability of an atom to attract the shared electrons to itself.
Electron affinity ($EA$) is the amount of energy released when an electron is added to a gaseous atom.
Ionisation energy ($IE$) is the amount of energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom.
Elements having high electron affinity easily lose electrons, resulting in the formation of cations.
Generally, the higher the first ionisation energy of an element, the greater is its electronegativity.
The strength of sharing or transferring valence electrons depends on the electronegativity of the atoms involved in the chemical bonding.
Forces between molecules, such as hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces, are known as strong forces.
London dispersion forces are the weakest intermolecular forces that can be found in all substances.
